2cf6d02e-5c41-40c6-8dea-2c5d9756b9c1.name: FDA
2cf6d02e-5c41-40c6-8dea-2c5d9756b9c1.parent: US Department of Health and Human Services (HHS)
keyOffices: Center for Food Safety and Applied Nutrition (CFSAN)
2cf6d02e-5c41-40c6-8dea-2c5d9756b9c1.description:
FDA regulates roughly 80% of the US food supply by value: packaged/processed foods, produce, seafood, dairy and shell eggs, bottled water, dietary supplements, wine under 7% ABV, and beer not made with both malted barley and hops.

Its authority is broader in scope but lighter-touch in presence than USDA FSIS's: rather than stationing an inspector at every regulated plant continuously, FSMA directs FDA to inspect high-risk facilities at least every 3 years and other facilities at least every 5 years, using registration and risk categorization (not physical presence) as the primary control. The practical consequence is that most day-to-day compliance assurance for FDA-regulated food comes from the facility's own PCHF-mandated Food Safety Plan and PCQI oversight, verified periodically by FDA inspection, rather than continuous federal oversight.

FDA's recall authority is mandatory under FSMA Section 423 for FDA-regulated food generally, with a separate, older mandatory mechanism specifically for infant formula (21 CFR Part 107) that predates and works differently from Section 423.

aac69997-d9a2-4428-860f-1d8813e9ba5a.name: USDA FSIS
aac69997-d9a2-4428-860f-1d8813e9ba5a.fullName: USDA Food Safety and Inspection Service
aac69997-d9a2-4428-860f-1d8813e9ba5a.description:
FSIS regulates meat, poultry, and processed egg products (not shell eggs, which fall to FDA). Its inspection model is the inverse of FDA's: rather than periodic risk-based visits, federal law requires a USDA inspector physically present during all hours slaughter is conducted (continuous, shift-by-shift inspection), and inspectors visit processing-only plants (further processing, not slaughter) at least once per operating day. This reflects FMIA/PPIA's older, presence-based statutory design rather than FSMA's newer risk-based approach.

Since 2013/2023 rule changes, most meat/poultry labels are self-certified under generic label approval rather than requiring FSIS pre-approval, which now applies only to special claims (e.g. "organic," "natural").

FSIS has no independent authority to compel a recall — every FSIS recall is voluntary, negotiated with the firm; if a firm refuses, FSIS's only lever is referring the matter to the Department of Justice for court-ordered detention or seizure of the product, a materially weaker enforcement posture than FDA's direct mandatory-recall authority under FSMA Section 423.

c8b293a1-17bc-42b2-b023-7d0c6dba905d.name: USDA AMS
c8b293a1-17bc-42b2-b023-7d0c6dba905d.description:
USDA's Agricultural Marketing Service runs programs that are opt-in rather than baseline safety law: quality grading (e.g. USDA Choice, USDA Grade A) is a voluntary, fee-based service a company requests, not a floor every facility must clear.

AMS also administers two disclosure regimes that function more like labeling law than grading: the National Organic Program, which accredits third-party certifying agents who in turn certify individual farms and handlers against the organic standard, and the National Bioengineered Food Disclosure Standard, which sets the disclosure format for bioengineered ingredients.

Because AMS's authority is program-based rather than a general food-safety mandate, a plant can be fully outside AMS's reach for grading purposes while still falling under FDA or FSIS for basic safety and labeling.

49089417-12e3-4123-8005-9e169c1ed9ce.name: EPA
49089417-12e3-4123-8005-9e169c1ed9ce.description:
EPA's reach into food manufacturing runs through chemical regulation rather than a food-safety mandate of its own. Under the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act, EPA sets the tolerances — maximum allowable pesticide residue levels — for raw agricultural commodities and processed foods; FDA and FSIS then enforce those tolerances during their own inspection and sampling programs.

EPA also regulates food-contact sanitizers and antimicrobial treatments as pesticides under FIFRA. Separately, EPA's drinking water standards under the Safe Drinking Water Act set the quality bar for water used as an ingredient or in sanitation, which matters to any plant on a municipal or private well supply.

Enforcement is largely upstream of the manufacturing floor — registration and tolerance-setting happen at the product/chemical level — so a plant's day-to-day EPA exposure is mostly about buying compliant sanitizers and staying under residue tolerances set elsewhere in the supply chain.

fa6a738b-947e-4ff9-a3cf-9b076e39a7a7.name: TTB
fa6a738b-947e-4ff9-a3cf-9b076e39a7a7.fullName: Alcohol and Tobacco Tax and Trade Bureau
fa6a738b-947e-4ff9-a3cf-9b076e39a7a7.description:
TTB is a Treasury Department bureau, not a food-safety agency — its authority over beverage alcohol comes from tax and trade law rather than product-safety law. Jurisdiction is split by a specific statutory line: TTB regulates beer brewed with both malted barley and hops, wine at 7% ABV or greater, and distilled spirits.

Products that fall outside that line — wine under 7% ABV, and fermented malt beverages made without both malted barley and hops — are FDA's responsibility instead, meaning two nominally similar beverage products can answer to two entirely different federal agencies depending on formulation and alcohol content.

Because TTB's mandate is tax and trade rather than food safety, a TTB-regulated facility is not thereby exempt from FDA or state sanitation and food-safety requirements that apply independently; TTB oversight is additive.

3c72b77c-84b1-4fc5-88bc-7a97193e2ffe.name: FTC
3c72b77c-84b1-4fc5-88bc-7a97193e2ffe.description:
The FTC is an independent federal agency whose consumer-protection mission covers deceptive or unfair advertising and marketing claims across all industries, food included. It does not regulate what appears on a food label itself — that is FDA's territory — but it does regulate how a food product is advertised: TV, print, digital, and social media claims, endorsements, and pricing/promotional practices.

The dividing line has been formalized since the 1970s in a liaison agreement between FTC and FDA: label and packaging claims fall to FDA, while an identical claim made in a magazine ad or a commercial falls to FTC.

Enforcement is civil, not inspection-based — the FTC has no field force that visits plants. Because its jurisdiction is advertising rather than the physical product, the FTC has essentially no touchpoints in the physical receiving-through-shipping flow this dataset models; its relevance here is mostly as a boundary marker explaining why label content and ad content are governed by different bodies.

57ebd4ad-028d-49a4-9840-805e858231d7.name: State & Local Agencies
57ebd4ad-028d-49a4-9840-805e858231d7.fullName: State departments of agriculture/health and local health departments
57ebd4ad-028d-49a4-9840-805e858231d7.description:
This is a catch-all for the layer of government below the federal agencies: state departments of agriculture and health, and county/municipal health departments. States are generally free to set food-safety and labeling requirements stricter than federal law, though federal preemption limits how far state rules can diverge in specific areas.

States and localities own several jobs the federal agencies don't do directly: licensing and routine inspection of retail food establishments and restaurants (FDA writes the Food Code as a model — adopted in some form by ~97% of states/territories — but has no authority to enforce it at a diner), some intrastate manufacturing licensing, weights-and-measures verification against NIST Handbook 44 as the federal reference model, and cottage food laws exempting small home-based producers (thresholds vary by state).

For a manufacturer already operating under FDA or FSIS oversight, state/local agencies are usually a secondary layer rather than the primary regulator, but they become primary for anything sold direct-to-consumer at retail or produced under an intrastate-only license.

2ba049e2-d8dd-4172-b13b-5830326ab4ad.name: CDC
2ba049e2-d8dd-4172-b13b-5830326ab4ad.fullName: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
2ba049e2-d8dd-4172-b13b-5830326ab4ad.parent: US Department of Health and Human Services (HHS)
2ba049e2-d8dd-4172-b13b-5830326ab4ad.description:
The CDC is the federal public-health agency responsible for detecting, investigating, and characterizing foodborne illness outbreaks after the fact — it has no regulatory or inspection authority over food facilities and cannot issue recalls or shut down a plant. Its role sits downstream of the manufacturing and distribution flow this dataset models: illness is reported by clinicians and state health departments, CDC aggregates and analyzes the case data, and it works jointly with FDA or USDA FSIS once a suspected food source is identified.

CDC also produces the periodic national burden-of-illness estimates: its current published headline estimate is ~48M illnesses, ~128,000 hospitalizations, and ~3,000 deaths annually, though the methodology behind it dates to 2011 (Scallan et al.) rather than a recent study. A narrower, more recent CDC estimate (2025) covers just 7 major pathogens for 2019 (~9.9M illnesses, ~53,300 hospitalizations, 931 deaths) and does not replace the broader 48M figure, which also covers unspecified agents.

While CDC touches no artifact or system in the receiving-to-shipping flow directly, its outbreak data is part of the evidentiary backdrop informing why traceability, sanitation, and allergen-control requirements exist in the form they do.

2ba804d3-9dc2-4848-94d1-f48c41ad9703.name: GS1
2ba804d3-9dc2-4848-94d1-f48c41ad9703.description:
GS1 is a global nonprofit standards organization, not a government body and not a regulator of any kind. It has no statutory authority and enforces nothing directly; its influence comes from the fact that retailers, distributors, and trading partners across the food supply chain widely use its identifier and data-exchange standards — including GTIN, GLN, SSCC, and EPCIS — as a common language for products, locations, and shipments.

GS1 standards intersect with food-safety regulation indirectly: FDA's Food Traceability Rule (FSMA 204) does not mandate GS1 specifically, but its required data elements (location identifiers, event data) map onto concepts GS1's standards already model.

3b121647-ec9f-444c-9084-8a6637d357da.name: GFSI
3b121647-ec9f-444c-9084-8a6637d357da.description:
GFSI (Global Food Safety Initiative) is a private-sector benchmarking body, not a certifying or auditing organization itself. It publishes benchmarking requirements that define what a food safety certification scheme must contain, and then reviews individual third-party schemes (SQF, BRCGS, FSSC 22000, IFS Food) against that document. A scheme that passes is declared "GFSI-recognized" — the actual audits and certificates are issued by the scheme owners and their accredited certification bodies, not by GFSI.

GFSI recognition matters commercially rather than legally: it has no government authority. Its leverage comes from major retailers and foodservice buyers requiring suppliers to hold a GFSI-recognized certification as a condition of doing business, layered on top of, not in place of, regulatory compliance.

Because GFSI-recognized schemes are built around HACCP, a facility already compliant with PCHF is usually most of the way toward meeting a GFSI scheme's requirements, though the audit covers additional management-system and prerequisite-program elements.

425f7abc-b53d-413d-b5ba-abfa92280e1c.name: OSHA
425f7abc-b53d-413d-b5ba-abfa92280e1c.description:
OSHA is a federal agency within the Department of Labor with authority over workplace safety and health conditions for nearly all private-sector employers, including food manufacturing plants. Its jurisdiction is the worker, not the product — a plant answers to FDA or USDA FSIS for product safety and to OSHA, separately and simultaneously, for conditions affecting employees.

Standards directly relevant to food plants include the General Duty Clause (requiring a workplace free of recognized hazards), machine guarding, lockout/tagout, personal protective equipment, the Hazard Communication Standard (chemical labeling, including sanitation and pest-control chemicals), and Process Safety Management for facilities with large anhydrous ammonia refrigeration inventories (common in cold storage/finished-goods warehousing). Enforcement runs through workplace inspections that can result in citations and civil penalties; many states also run their own OSHA-approved state plans.

Because OSHA and food-safety regulators inspect the same physical plant for different purposes, their requirements can touch the same equipment or process from different angles without either agency deferring to the other.

9c1e2f3a-4b5c-4d6e-8f7a-1b2c3d4e5f6a.name: CBP
9c1e2f3a-4b5c-4d6e-8f7a-1b2c3d4e5f6a.description:
CBP is the Department of Homeland Security agency responsible for administering entry of goods across U.S. borders, including all imported food. When a shipment of FDA-regulated food arrives, the importer (or its broker) files entry documents with CBP, which refers those entries to FDA for an admissibility decision — CBP itself does not decide whether a food shipment is safe or compliant.

CBP holds the physical and legal levers of border control: it can detain, hold, or refuse to release a shipment based on FDA's determination, and it collects duties and enforces customs law independent of food safety. This is a border-enforcement/logistics role distinct from FDA's FSVP, which places hazard-analysis and verification duties on the US importer itself — the shipment physically moves through CBP's entry process even though FSVP compliance is verified and enforced by FDA.
